subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 2! In this unit you will become an explorer of shapes. Shapes are everywhere around you, and once you start to notice them you will never stop. The round moon that tells us when Ramadan begins, the square tiles on the masjid floor, the tall cylinder of a water tank in Hargeisa, the flat triangle of a folded shawl – all of these are shapes with special names and special properties. In this unit you will learn to name them, describe them, and understand how they fit together.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 2 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Identify and name common 3D shapes: sphere, cube, cuboid, cylinder and square-based pyramid.
Describe 3D shapes using the words faces, edges and vertices, and recognise curved surfaces.
Spot 3D shapes in everyday objects around your home and neighbourhood.
Identify and name 2D shapes, including the new polygons pentagon, hexagon and octagon.
Find and draw lines of symmetry (vertical and horizontal) on 2D shapes.
Create your own symmetrical patterns and designs.
Divide shapes into equal parts and name halves, quarters and three-quarters.
Tell the difference between equal and unequal parts of a whole.
Use 3D shape knowledge to work out what a shape looks like from above (its footprint).

concepts.title: What Are 3D Shapes?
concepts.explanation: A 3D shape is a solid shape. The letters '3D' stand for three dimensions: length, width and height. A 3D shape takes up space and you can hold it in your hand. Think of a football, a sugar cube, or a tin of camel milk – each of these is solid and takes up room on the shelf. In this unit you will meet five important 3D shapes: the sphere, the cube, the cuboid, the cylinder, and the square-based pyramid. Let us look at each one carefully and learn what makes it special.
concepts.example: A sphere is perfectly round, like a ball. It has no flat faces, no edges and no vertices. Instead it has just one curved surface that goes all the way around. If you roll a sphere it moves smoothly in any direction,…

concepts.title: Faces, Edges and Vertices
concepts.explanation: To describe a 3D shape well, we use three special words: faces, edges and vertices. Learning these three words carefully is the key to the whole of 3D geometry, so let us take them one at a time. A face is a flat surface of a 3D shape. If you place a cube on the table, the part touching the table is one face, the part on top is another, and there are four faces around the sides – 6 faces in all. A face is always flat; a rounded surface does not count as a face.
concepts.example: An edge is the straight line where two faces meet. Run your finger along the top of a box; that sharp line is an edge. A cube has 12 edges – four around the top, four around the bottom, and four standing up the sides.…

concepts.title: 3D Shapes in Our World
concepts.explanation: The best way to learn 3D shapes is to hunt for them in real life. Once you know the five shapes, you will see them everywhere – in the kitchen, at the market, in the masjid, and out in the fields. Let us go looking together. Spheres: a football in the yard, an orange or a ripe mango, a marble, the round beads on a tasbih.
concepts.example: Cubes: a dice, a sugar cube, a child's wooden block, a small gift box that is equal on every side.

concepts.title: 2D Shapes and Polygons
concepts.explanation: Now we move from solid shapes to flat shapes. A 2D shape is flat and has only length and width. You already know some 2D shapes very well, and in this unit you will meet three brand-new ones. Let us begin with the shapes you know: Circle: a perfectly round flat shape. Every point on its edge is the same distance from the centre.
concepts.example: Triangle: a shape with 3 straight sides and 3 vertices (corners).

concepts.title: Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
concepts.explanation: Symmetry is one of the most beautiful ideas in all of mathematics. A shape has symmetry when it can be divided into two parts that are exact mirror images of each other. The line that divides the shape into two matching halves is called a line of symmetry. Imagine folding a shape in half along a line. If the two halves land exactly on top of each other, with no part sticking out, then that fold line is a line of symmetry. This is why folding paper is the simplest way to test for symmetry. Khadija folds a paper…
concepts.example: There are two lines of symmetry we name by their direction:

concepts.title: Turning and Flipping Shapes
concepts.explanation: The same shape can look different depending on how it is turned or flipped. This is an important idea, because a triangle standing on its point looks different from one sitting on its base – yet it is still the same triangle. When we turn a shape around, we call it a rotation. When we flip a shape over, like turning a page, we call it a reflection. Some shapes look exactly the same no matter how you turn them: a circle always looks like a circle, however you spin it. Other shapes look different when turned: a…
concepts.example: Here is something worth thinking about carefully. If Omar turns a square a little, it may look like a diamond, but it is still a square – the sides and corners have not changed, only the direction it faces. Learning to…

methods.title: How to describe a 3D shape
methods.example: A tin of tomatoes has two circular flat ends and one curved surface around the middle. What 3D shape is it?
steps: Count the flat faces (do not count curved surfaces).
Count the edges – the lines where faces meet.
Count the vertices – the corner points.
Say the shape's name and its three numbers.

methods.title: How to find a line of symmetry
methods.example: How many faces, edges and vertices does a cube have?
steps: Imagine folding the shape in half along a line.
Check whether the two halves match exactly.
If they match, that line is a line of symmetry.
Try vertical, horizontal and diagonal lines to find them all.

methods.title: How to divide a shape into equal parts
methods.example: A cube is sitting on a table. One face is flat on the table and hidden. How many faces can you see?
steps: Decide how many equal parts you need (2 for halves, 4 for quarters).
Draw lines so that every part is the same size.
Check the parts are equal by comparing them.
Name each part: one-half, one-quarter, or three-quarters.

methods.title: Name the polygon
methods.example: Sagal draws a closed shape with 6 straight sides. What is it called?
steps: Solution: A closed shape with 6 straight sides is a hexagon.
(Remember: 5 sides is a pentagon, 6 is a hexagon, 8 is an octagon.)
Check the name the polygon result carefully.

methods.title: Count lines of symmetry
methods.example: How many lines of symmetry does a square have?
steps: Solution: A square has 4 lines of symmetry: one vertical, one horizontal, and two diagonal.
You can fold it along any of these four lines and the halves will match exactly.
Worked Example 6: Halves or not?
Noor cuts a rectangle into two pieces.

methods.title: Halves or not?
methods.example: How many lines of symmetry does a square have?
steps: Solution: A square has 4 lines of symmetry: one vertical, one horizontal, and two diagonal.
You can fold it along any of these four lines and the halves will match exactly.
Worked Example 6: Halves or not?
Noor cuts a rectangle into two pieces.

workedExamples.title: Name the 3D shape
workedExamples.prompt: A tin of tomatoes has two circular flat ends and one curved surface around the middle. What 3D shape is it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Two flat circular faces joined by a curved surface, with no corners, describes a cylinder. So the tin is a cylinder.

workedExamples.title: Count faces, edges and vertices
workedExamples.prompt: How many faces, edges and vertices does a cube have?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count slowly. A cube has 6 faces (top, bottom, and four sides), 12 edges (four around the top, four around the bottom, four standing up), and 8 vertices (one at each corner). So the answer is 6 faces, 12 edges, 8 vertices.

workedExamples.title: Hidden faces
workedExamples.prompt: A cube is sitting on a table. One face is flat on the table and hidden. How many faces can you see?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A cube has 6 faces in total. One face is hidden against the table. So you can see 6 − 1 = 5 faces.

workedExamples.title: Name the polygon
workedExamples.prompt: Sagal draws a closed shape with 6 straight sides. What is it called?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A closed shape with 6 straight sides is a hexagon. (Remember: 5 sides is a pentagon, 6 is a hexagon, 8 is an octagon.)

workedExamples.title: Count lines of symmetry
workedExamples.prompt: How many lines of symmetry does a square have?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A square has 4 lines of symmetry: one vertical, one horizontal, and two diagonal. You can fold it along any of these four lines and the halves will match exactly. Worked Example 6: Halves or not? Noor cuts a rectangle into two pieces. One piece is large and one is small. Has she made halves? Solution: No. To make halves, the two parts must be exactly equal in size. Because one piece is large and one is small, they are unequal parts, not halves.

workedExamples.title: Halves or not?
workedExamples.prompt: How many lines of symmetry does a square have?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A square has 4 lines of symmetry: one vertical, one horizontal, and two diagonal. You can fold it along any of these four lines and the halves will match exactly. Worked Example 6: Halves or not? Noor cuts a rectangle into two pieces. One piece is large and one is small. Has she made halves? Solution: No. To make halves, the two parts must be exactly equal in size. Because one piece is large and one is small, they are unequal parts, not halves.

workedExamples.title: Sharing into quarters
workedExamples.prompt: Four children share a square pizza equally. What fraction does each child get, and how should the pizza be cut?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Four equal shares means quarters. Cut the pizza with one line down and one line across to make 4 equal parts. Each child gets one-quarter (¼) of the pizza.

workedExamples.title: Shape from above
workedExamples.prompt: Liban looks straight down on a cylinder standing on its end. What 2D shape does he see?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Looking down on a cylinder, he sees its circular top face, so the footprint is a circle.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: A shape is cut into 2 equal parts. What is each part called? What about 4 equal parts?
workedExamples.solution: 2 equal parts = halves; 4 equal parts = quarters.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Name the curved-surface shapes among: cube, sphere, cylinder, cuboid.
workedExamples.solution: Sphere and cylinder have curved surfaces.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: How many faces are hidden when a cuboid sits on its largest face?
workedExamples.solution: 5 faces hidden? No – 1 face is hidden (the one on the table); 5 are visible.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: A regular hexagon has how many lines of symmetry? What about a regular octagon?
workedExamples.solution: 1) Regular hexagon has 6 lines of symmetry; regular octagon has 8.

activities.title: 3D Shape Hunt
activities.materials: your home, and a pencil and paper to record what you find.
steps: Walk through the kitchen and living room looking for 3D shapes.
Find at least one sphere, one cube, one cuboid and one cylinder.
Write the name of each object next to its shape name.
Ask a family member to add one more object to each shape group.
Keep your list. You will be surprised how many shapes hide in an ordinary room!

activities.title: Face Printing
activities.materials: a few solid objects (a tin, a box, a block), some paint or a stamp pad, and paper.
steps: Choose one object and press one flat face onto paper to print its shape.
Name the 2D shape you printed (a square, a rectangle, a circle, a triangle).
Try every different face of the object and print each one.
Say which 3D shape each print came from.
This shows you how the flat faces of 3D shapes are really 2D shapes.

activities.title: The Symmetry Mirror Game
activities.materials: a small mirror (or shiny spoon), paper, and a pencil.
steps: Draw a shape such as a square, a heart, or a butterfly.
Stand the mirror upright on a line through the middle of the shape.
If it matches, you have found a line of symmetry. Try other lines too.
This game trains your eye to spot symmetry quickly – a skill artists and tailors use every day.

activities.title: Fair Sharing Investigation
activities.materials: a round anjero or a square piece of bread, and a knife (ask an adult to help).
steps: Start with one whole piece of food and estimate how to cut it fairly for two people.
Cut it into 2 equal parts. Check that the parts are the same size – these are halves.
Now cut each half again to make 4 equal parts – these are quarters.
Talk about what happens to the size of each part as you cut more pieces.
You have just turned sharing food into a lesson about fractions and equal parts.

activities.title: Shape Guessing Game (Family Game)
steps: One player secretly chooses a 3D or 2D shape.
That player describes it without saying its name: ‘I have 6 square faces and 8 corners.’
Everyone else guesses the shape from the clues.
Take turns so everyone has a chance to describe and to guess.
Describing shapes with the right words – faces, edges, vertices, sides – makes the game fair and helps everyone learn.

activities.title: Build a Shape With Cubes
activities.materials: four small cubes (sugar cubes, blocks, or clay cubes you make yourself).
steps: Use all four cubes to build a shape – a tower, an L-shape, or a square.
Write down how you made it, using words like 'on top of' and 'next to'.
Read your instructions to a partner and see if they can build the same shape.
Did your partner use the correct words? How could the description be clearer?

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand shapes best? What was the trickiest part – counting edges, finding symmetry, or making equal parts? What would you like more practice with? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Faces, Edges and Vertices Rule
rules.text: A face is a FLAT surface. A curved surface is not a face. An edge is a line where two faces meet. A vertex is a corner point where edges meet.

rules.title: Polygon Rule
rules.text: A polygon is a closed 2D shape with only straight sides. Count the sides to name it: 5 = pentagon, 6 = hexagon, 8 = octagon. A circle is not a polygon because its edge is curved.

rules.title: Fraction Rule
rules.text: The parts must be EQUAL in size. Two equal parts = halves. Four equal parts = quarters. Four quarters make one whole; three-quarters means 3 of 4 parts.

terms: A solid shape you can hold
Flat surface / line join / corner point
A rounded surface that is not flat
A closed 2D shape with straight sides
The fold line making two matching halves
One of 2 / one of 4 equal parts
commonMistakes: Counting a curved surface as a face
Both parts must be the same size
A turned square is still a square

reasoningPrompts.prompt: From above, a shape looks like a square. Name two different 3D shapes it could be.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A cube and a square-based pyramid.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Waris says every triangle has 3 lines of symmetry. Is this always, sometimes, or never true? Explain with an example.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Sometimes true – an equilateral triangle has 3 lines of symmetry, but an isosceles triangle has only 1 and a scalene triangle has none.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A cube is dipped in paint on one face and stamped on paper. What 2D shape is printed? What if the end of a cylinder is stamped?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A cube face prints a square; a cylinder end prints a circle.

questions.question: What does “3D shape” mean?
options: A solid shape you can hold
Flat surface / line join / corner point
A rounded surface that is not flat
A closed 2D shape with straight sides
questions.answer: A solid shape you can hold
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: 3D shape means A solid shape you can hold.

options: Flat surface / line join / corner point
A rounded surface that is not flat
A closed 2D shape with straight sides
questions.answer: Flat surface / line join / corner point
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Face / Edge / Vertex means Flat surface / line join / corner point.

questions.question: What does “Curved surface” mean?

selfAssessment: I can identify and name common 3D shapes: sphere, cube, cuboid, cylinder and square-based pyramid.
I can describe 3D shapes using the words faces, edges and vertices, and recognise curved surfaces.
I can spot 3D shapes in everyday objects around your home and neighbourhood.
I can identify and name 2D shapes, including the new polygons pentagon, hexagon and octagon.
I can find and draw lines of symmetry (vertical and horizontal) on 2D shapes.
I can create your own symmetrical patterns and designs.
I can divide shapes into equal parts and name halves, quarters and three-quarters.
I can tell the difference between equal and unequal parts of a whole.
